_meta.description: Theory of Mind taxonomy for SemEval-2025 Task 10. NARRATIVE level: multi-class mechanisms (a narrative may span multiple cognitive mechanisms). SUB-NARRATIVE level: single-class mechanism (each concrete claim has one primary cognitive function).
_meta.status: Pre-filled from literature, requires domain expert validation

mechanisms.epistemic: Changing what the reader believes is TRUE — targeting factual beliefs, trust in sources, causal models. Grounding: Harris (2023) types a/b.
mechanisms.emotional: Triggering fear, hope, sympathy, outrage, anxiety, or despair to bypass rational evaluation. Grounding: Wilbur (2025), Petty & Cacioppo ELM.
mechanisms.identity: Reinforcing in-group / out-group affiliation, us-vs-them framing. Grounding: Kahan et al. (2007), Feygina et al. (2010).
mechanisms.moral: Framing actors as good / evil, hero / villain, competent / incompetent. Grounding: Entity Framing ST1 taxonomy, Haidt moral foundations.

grounding_key.Harris_2023: Three disinformation effects: (a) false beliefs, (b) prevent true beliefs, (c) sub-doxastic
grounding_key.Cook_2020: FLICC→psychology mapping; CARDS negates 5 climate beliefs (Ding 2011)
grounding_key.Wilbur_2025: Rhetorical structure → cognitive bias on 260 Russian disinfo texts
grounding_key.Maillat_Oswald_2009: Context Selection Constraint model of manipulation
grounding_key.ReflexiveControl: Soviet framework: making target 'independently' decide in controller's favour
grounding_key.Campbell_Kay_2014: Solution aversion — denying problem to avoid threatening solutions
grounding_key.Pauli_2022: Propaganda techniques as misuse of ethos/pathos/logos

Blaming the war on others rather than th.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may hold Russia partially responsible for initiating the conflict
Blaming the war on others rather than th.target_belief_change: Responsibility for the war lies with Ukraine or the West, not Russia
Blaming the war on others rather than th.persuasive_strategy: Causal reattribution — shifting perceived agency and blame
Blaming the war on others rather than th.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a (false causal beliefs); ReflexiveControl; Pauli_2022 misuse of logos
Blaming the war on others rather than th.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative re-attributes causality. Look for language shifting blame away from Russia toward Ukraine, NATO, or the West.

Ukraine is the aggressor.tom_note: Reverses aggressor role — claims Ukraine provoked or started the conflict
Ukraine is the aggressor.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing false belief about who attacked whom; ReflexiveControl: making audience conclude Russia was provoked

The West are the aggressors.tom_note: Attributes causality to Western actions (NATO expansion, Minsk sabotage)
The West are the aggressors.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: false causal attribution; Maillat_Oswald_2009: redirecting contextual assumptions about agency

Discrediting Ukraine.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may have baseline sympathy or respect for Ukraine
Discrediting Ukraine.target_belief_change: Ukraine is unworthy of support — corrupt, criminal, extremist, or illegitimate
Discrediting Ukraine.persuasive_strategy: Character assassination — undermining moral standing through multiple attack vectors
Discrediting Ukraine.grounding: EntityFramingST1 (Ukraine as Antagonist); Pauli_2022 misuse of ethos; Maillat_Oswald_2009
Discrediting Ukraine.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks Ukraine's moral standing through diverse sub-claims. Each sub-narrative uses a different cognitive lever.

Rewriting Ukraine's history.tom_note: Targets factual beliefs about Ukrainian nationhood and historical legitimacy
Rewriting Ukraine's history.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing false historical beliefs; Stanley_2015: manipulating presuppositions about national identity

Discrediting Ukrainian nation and societ.tom_note: Attacks the moral character of Ukrainian people as a whole — corruption, cultural deficiency
Discrediting Ukrainian nation and societ.grounding: EntityFramingST1: casting Ukrainian people as Antagonist (Corrupt, Bigot); Pauli_2022: misuse of ethos (character attack on collective)

Discrediting Ukrainian military.tom_note: Attacks the competence and conduct of Ukrainian armed forces
Discrediting Ukrainian military.grounding: EntityFramingST1: Ukrainian military as Antagonist (Incompetent, Terrorist); annotation guidelines: often overlaps with 'Praise of Russian military might'

Discrediting Ukrainian government and of.tom_note: Attacks the legitimacy, competence, or integrity of Ukraine's leadership
Discrediting Ukrainian government and of.grounding: EntityFramingST1: Zelensky/officials as Antagonist (Tyrant, Corrupt, Deceiver); Pauli_2022: misuse of ethos

Ukraine is a puppet of the West.tom_note: Claims Ukraine lacks sovereignty — targets beliefs about Ukraine's agency and decision-making autonomy
Ukraine is a puppet of the West.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about power dynamics; ReflexiveControl; annotation guidelines: prefer over 'West doesn't care' when focus is on Ukraine's legitimacy

Ukraine is a hub for criminal activities.tom_note: Frames Ukraine as morally corrupt through association with criminality
Ukraine is a hub for criminal activities.grounding: EntityFramingST1: Ukraine as Antagonist (Corrupt); moral contamination through criminal association

Ukraine is associated with nazism.tom_note: Triggers deep identity-based rejection through association with the most extreme out-group marker
Ukraine is associated with nazism.grounding: Kahan_2007: identity-protective cognition — nazism as ultimate out-group marker; annotation guidelines: 'should be used with ANY mention of (neo-)Nazism, historical or not'

Situation in Ukraine is hopeless.tom_note: Induces despair and futility about Ukraine's future to undermine support
Situation in Ukraine is hopeless.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion — framing Ukraine's situation as irreversible loss; ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (despair)

Russia is the Victim.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may view Russia as the aggressor
Russia is the Victim.target_belief_change: Russia is unfairly treated and deserves sympathy
Russia is the Victim.grounding: EntityFramingST1 (Russia as Innocent/Victim); Wilbur_2025 emotional contagion; Bondarenko_2020 emotional appeals
Russia is the Victim.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative inverts the aggressor/victim frame. Look for language evoking sympathy for Russia or framing Western responses as persecution.

The West is russophobic.tom_note: Reframes criticism of Russia's actions as cultural prejudice against Russians as a group
The West is russophobic.grounding: Kahan_2007: identity-protective cognition — frames anti-Russia sentiment as identity-based prejudice rather than policy response; Stanley_2015: exploiting identity presuppositions

Russia actions in Ukraine are only self-.tom_note: Reframes invasion as legitimate defensive action — targets factual beliefs about causality and legality
Russia actions in Ukraine are only self-.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about the nature of Russia's military action; ReflexiveControl: making audience conclude Russia had no choice

UA is anti-RU extremists.tom_note: Frames Ukraine as comprising extremist elements hostile to Russians — activates in-group/out-group dynamics
UA is anti-RU extremists.grounding: Kahan_2007: identity-protective cognition; EntityFramingST1: Ukraine as Antagonist (Terrorist, Bigot)

Praise of Russia.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may view Russia negatively or be uncertain about its strength and legitimacy
Praise of Russia.target_belief_change: Russia is strong, admirable, internationally supported, and a legitimate alternative to Western hegemony
Praise of Russia.persuasive_strategy: Positive identity construction — building in-group pride and projecting strength
Praise of Russia.grounding: Kahan_2007 identity alignment; EntityFramingST1 (Russia as Protagonist); ReflexiveControl: projecting strength
Praise of Russia.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative constructs a positive image of Russia. Look for glorification of military, leadership, diplomacy, or international standing.

Praise of Russian military might.tom_note: Projects military strength to inspire alignment with the powerful side
Praise of Russian military might.grounding: Kahan_2007: aligning with the 'winning' side; EntityFramingST1: Russian military as Protagonist (Guardian); annotation guidelines: often overlaps with 'Discrediting Ukrainian military'

Praise of Russian President Vladimir Put.tom_note: Frames Putin as a strong, decisive, admirable leader — hero/great-man narrative
Praise of Russian President Vladimir Put.grounding: EntityFramingST1: Putin as Protagonist (Guardian, Virtuous); Pauli_2022: appeal to ethos through leader glorification

Russia is a guarantor of peace and prosp.tom_note: Frames Russia as a benevolent protector — hero/guardian moral framing
Russia is a guarantor of peace and prosp.grounding: EntityFramingST1: Russia as Protagonist (Guardian, Peacemaker); contrasts with Western antagonist framing

Russia has international support from a.tom_note: Factual claim about breadth of international support — targets beliefs about Russia's isolation
Russia has international support from a.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing (possibly exaggerated) beliefs about support levels; Wilbur_2025: bandwagon effect

Russian invasion has strong national sup.tom_note: Emphasises in-group solidarity within Russia — 'the people support it'
Russian invasion has strong national sup.grounding: Kahan_2007: in-group solidarity signalling; annotation guidelines: 'Use only when there is mention of the Russian population'

Overpraising the West.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may be sceptical of Western motives or effectiveness
Overpraising the West.target_belief_change: Western nations are morally superior, militarily dominant, and historically justified
Overpraising the West.persuasive_strategy: In-group glorification — excessive lauding of Western values and capabilities
Overpraising the West.grounding: Feygina_2010 system justification; Kahan_2007; Pauli_2022 flag-waving
Overpraising the West.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative excessively praises Western actions and moral standing. Can function as counter-propaganda or genuine overconfidence.

NATO will destroy Russia.tom_note: Combines glorification with implied threat — triggers confidence or fear of escalation depending on reader
NATO will destroy Russia.grounding: Wilbur_2025: anchoring bias (inflated military capability claims); dual reading: reassurance for Western audience / threat for Russian audience

The West belongs in the right side of hi.tom_note: Claims moral superiority and historical inevitability for Western values
The West belongs in the right side of hi.grounding: Feygina_2010: system justification — defending Western-led order as morally correct; Stanley_2015: ideological common ground

The West has the strongest international.tom_note: Factual claim about breadth of international backing for Western position
The West has the strongest international.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing (possibly exaggerated) beliefs about support; mirrors 'Russia has international support' from opposite side

Speculating war outcomes.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader is uncertain about how the war will develop
Speculating war outcomes.target_belief_change: One side is inevitably winning/losing — the outcome is predetermined
Speculating war outcomes.persuasive_strategy: Deterministic framing — creating inevitability narratives to shape expectations and morale
Speculating war outcomes.grounding: Wilbur_2025 anchoring bias; Bondarenko_2020 information restructuring; ARTT_2022 manufacturing doubt
Speculating war outcomes.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative speculates about military outcomes. Look for predictions of collapse, inevitable victory/defeat, or deterministic framing.

Russian army is collapsing.tom_note: Presents speculative military assessment as factual — targets beliefs about Russian military capability
Russian army is collapsing.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing false beliefs about military status; Wilbur_2025: anchoring on dramatic claims

Russian army will lose all the occupied.tom_note: Predictive claim about territorial outcomes — targets beliefs about war trajectory
Russian army will lose all the occupied.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: speculative prediction presented as inevitable outcome

Ukrainian army is collapsing.tom_note: Presents speculative military assessment of Ukrainian forces as factual — targets beliefs about military viability
Ukrainian army is collapsing.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: speculative prediction presented as factual; Didi decision: symmetric with 'Russian army is collapsing' — both are epistemic military predictions regardless of which side

Discrediting the West, Diplomacy.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may trust Western institutions and diplomatic processes
Discrediting the West, Diplomacy.target_belief_change: Western institutions are hypocritical, divided, incompetent, self-interested, and diplomacy is futile
Discrediting the West, Diplomacy.persuasive_strategy: Institutional trust erosion through exposure of failures and contradictions
Discrediting the West, Diplomacy.grounding: Sperber_2010 undermining epistemic vigilance; ReflexiveControl: weakening alliance cohesion; Pauli_2022 misuse of ethos
Discrediting the West, Diplomacy.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative erodes trust in Western institutions and diplomacy. Look for language suggesting incompetence, hypocrisy, division, war-weariness, or self-interest.

The EU is divided.tom_note: Factual/exaggerated claim about EU internal disagreements — targets beliefs about collective Western resolve
The EU is divided.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a/b: (exaggerated) beliefs about EU disunity; ReflexiveControl: exploiting real divisions to amplify perception of weakness

The West is weak.tom_note: Factual claim about Western decline in power — targets beliefs about Western capability
The West is weak.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing beliefs about Western decline; Bondarenko_2020: information restructuring (re-scaling Western power)

The West is overreacting.tom_note: Proportionality judgment — claims Western response is disproportionate to the situation
The West is overreacting.grounding: Maillat_Oswald_2009: manipulating contextual assumptions about proportionality; targets the belief that sanctions/support are appropriate

The West does not care about Ukraine, on.tom_note: Claims about Western motives — targets beliefs about Western sincerity in supporting Ukraine
The West does not care about Ukraine, on.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: false/exaggerated beliefs about Western motivation; annotation guidelines: prefer 'puppet of West' when focus is on Ukraine's legitimacy

Diplomacy does/will not work.tom_note: Induces futility and despair about peaceful resolution — emotionally weighted toward helplessness
Diplomacy does/will not work.grounding: ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (futility); Wilbur_2025: loss aversion — framing diplomatic options as exhausted

West is tired of Ukraine.tom_note: Induces abandonment anxiety — emotionally suggests Western support will fade
West is tired of Ukraine.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion (impending loss of support); ReflexiveControl: undermining adversary's belief in sustained alliance commitment

Negative Consequences for the West.presupposed_reader_belief: Western reader may believe supporting Ukraine serves their interests or at minimum doesn't harm them
Negative Consequences for the West.target_belief_change: Supporting Ukraine will directly harm the reader's own economic wellbeing and security
Negative Consequences for the West.persuasive_strategy: Self-interest appeal through fear — making costs of Ukraine support personally salient
Negative Consequences for the West.grounding: Wilbur_2025 loss aversion; Campbell_Kay_2014 solution aversion; ARTT_2022 evoking emotion
Negative Consequences for the West.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative makes the war's costs personal to Western readers. Look for language about sanctions backfiring, energy crises, refugee flows, or economic decline.

Sanctions imposed by Western countries w.tom_note: Triggers economic fear — sanctions will hurt 'us' more than Russia
Sanctions imposed by Western countries w.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion (economic losses); Campbell_Kay_2014: solution aversion — rejecting the policy because its costs are threatening

The conflict will increase the Ukrainian.tom_note: Triggers anxiety about migration — combines economic fear with potential xenophobic undertones
The conflict will increase the Ukrainian.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion + emotional contagion; ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (fear of societal disruption); Pauli_2022: appeal to prejudice

Distrust towards Media.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may rely on Western or Ukrainian media for information about the conflict
Distrust towards Media.target_belief_change: The reader's trusted media sources are propaganda instruments — doubt everything from these sources
Distrust towards Media.persuasive_strategy: Meta-epistemic attack — destroying trust in the reader's entire information ecosystem
Distrust towards Media.grounding: Harris_2023 type-b/c (preventing true beliefs + sub-doxastic uncertainty); Sperber_2010 attacking epistemic vigilance; ARTT_2022 manufacturing doubt
Distrust towards Media.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks the reader's information sources. Look for language discrediting Western or Ukrainian media as propaganda, biased, or controlled.

Western media is an instrument of propag.tom_note: Attacks credibility of Western media as a whole — meta-epistemic (targets the source, not specific claims)
Western media is an instrument of propag.grounding: Sperber_2010: directly attacking epistemic vigilance toward Western information sources; Harris_2023 type-b: preventing formation of true beliefs by destroying source trust

Ukrainian media cannot be trusted.tom_note: Attacks credibility of Ukrainian media specifically — targets beliefs about wartime reporting reliability
Ukrainian media cannot be trusted.grounding: Sperber_2010: attacking epistemic vigilance toward Ukrainian sources; ARTT_2022: discrediting

Amplifying war-related fears.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader values peace and personal safety; may feel distant from the conflict
Amplifying war-related fears.target_belief_change: The conflict will escalate catastrophically and directly threaten the reader's safety
Amplifying war-related fears.persuasive_strategy: Fear amplification through catastrophic scenario framing
Amplifying war-related fears.grounding: Wilbur_2025 emotional contagion + loss aversion; ARTT_2022 evoking emotion; Pauli_2022 appeal to fear
Amplifying war-related fears.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative exploits fear of escalation. Look for language about WWIII, nuclear weapons, NATO intervention, or war spreading.

By continuing the war we risk WWIII.tom_note: Existential fear of global war — frames continued Ukraine support as path to world war
By continuing the war we risk WWIII.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion (existential scale); ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (existential fear)

Russia will also attack other countries.tom_note: Expansion fear — the conflict will spread geographically toward the reader
Russia will also attack other countries.grounding: Wilbur_2025: emotional contagion (geographic proximity of threat); Maillat_Oswald_2009: manipulating threat proximity assumptions

There is a real possibility that nuclear.tom_note: Nuclear fear — the ultimate existential threat. Annotation guidelines: can go two directions (don't anger Russia OR stop Russia before they use them)
There is a real possibility that nuclear.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion at civilisational scale; annotation guidelines: bidirectional framing

NATO should/will directly intervene.tom_note: Escalation fear through direct military confrontation between nuclear powers
NATO should/will directly intervene.grounding: ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (fear of direct confrontation); overlaps with epistemic when presenting intervention as strategic necessity

Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may accept official explanations for the conflict's causes
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.target_belief_change: Hidden elites control events — official narratives are covers for secret agendas
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.persuasive_strategy: Conspiratorial framing — replacing transparent explanations with hidden-agent narratives
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.grounding: Cook_2020 conspiracy theory characteristics; ARTT_2022 conspiratorial reasoning; Bondarenko_2020 information restructuring
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative invokes secret plots and hidden agendas. Look for language about elites, secret plans, or claims the war is a cover for something bigger.
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.sub_narratives: _note: No sub-narratives defined in the SemEval-2025 Task 10 taxonomy for URW hidden plots. The 'Other' sub-narrative captures all instances.

Criticism of climate policies.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may support current climate policies as necessary
Criticism of climate policies.target_belief_change: Climate policies are ineffective, economically harmful, or profit-driven
Criticism of climate policies.persuasive_strategy: Policy delegitimisation — attacking the solution without necessarily denying the problem
Criticism of climate policies.grounding: Campbell_Kay_2014 solution aversion; Cook_2020 CARDS 'solutions won't work'; ARTT_2022 manufacturing doubt
Criticism of climate policies.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks climate policies, not climate science. Look for claims policies are ineffective, too costly, or driven by profit.

Climate policies are ineffective.tom_note: Targets factual beliefs about policy effectiveness — claims policies don't achieve their stated goals
Climate policies are ineffective.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about policy outcomes; Campbell_Kay_2014: solution aversion; Cook_2020: CARDS 'solutions won't work'

Climate policies have negative impact on.tom_note: Triggers economic anxiety — frames climate action as personally costly to the reader
Climate policies have negative impact on.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion (economic losses from policy); Campbell_Kay_2014: solution aversion driven by economic threat

Climate policies are only for profit.tom_note: Frames policy advocates as morally corrupt — driven by greed rather than environmental concern
Climate policies are only for profit.grounding: EntityFramingST1: policy advocates as Antagonist (Corrupt); Pauli_2022: misuse of ethos (attacking advocate motives)

Criticism of institutions and authoritie.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may trust climate-relevant institutions (EU, UN, IPCC, governments)
Criticism of institutions and authoritie.target_belief_change: Climate institutions are incompetent, overreaching, or ideologically driven
Criticism of institutions and authoritie.persuasive_strategy: Institutional trust erosion — parallels URW 'Discrediting the West'
Criticism of institutions and authoritie.grounding: Sperber_2010 undermining epistemic vigilance; Pauli_2022 misuse of ethos
Criticism of institutions and authoritie.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks climate institutions, not the science or policy itself. Look for language discrediting EU, UN, IPCC, governments, or political figures.

Criticism of the EU.tom_note: Targets trust in EU as climate policy-maker — claims EU overreaches or imposes arbitrary rules
Criticism of the EU.grounding: Sperber_2010: undermining institutional epistemic authority; parallels URW 'EU is divided'

Criticism of international entities.tom_note: Targets trust in international bodies (UN, IPCC) — claims they are mandating unfounded changes
Criticism of international entities.grounding: Sperber_2010; Cook_2020: attacking the messenger (FLICC technique applied to institutions)

Criticism of national governments.tom_note: Targets trust in national-level climate policy — claims government lacks competence or plan
Criticism of national governments.grounding: Sperber_2010; Harris_2023 type-b: preventing trust in governmental climate action

Criticism of political organizations and.tom_note: Personal/organisational character attack — targets specific political actors' credibility on climate
Criticism of political organizations and.grounding: EntityFramingST1: political figures as Antagonist (Incompetent, Deceiver); Pauli_2022: ad hominem / misuse of ethos

Climate change is beneficial.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader believes climate change is predominantly harmful
Climate change is beneficial.target_belief_change: Climate change has positive effects that are being suppressed or ignored
Climate change is beneficial.persuasive_strategy: Counter-narrative through selective evidence — cherry-picking positive effects
Climate change is beneficial.grounding: Cook_2020: cherry picking (FLICC technique); Harris_2023 type-a; challenges Ding 2011 belief 'it's bad'
Climate change is beneficial.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative reframes climate change as having positive aspects. Look for language about benefits of CO2 or warmer temperatures.

CO2 is beneficial.tom_note: Counter-factual claim that increased CO2 has positive environmental effects
CO2 is beneficial.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: producing false belief about net CO2 effects; Cook_2020: cherry picking positive studies

Temperature increase is beneficial.tom_note: Counter-factual claim that warming produces net benefits (longer seasons, etc.)
Temperature increase is beneficial.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a; Cook_2020: cherry picking — selecting one positive effect to counter overwhelming negative evidence

Downplaying climate change.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader believes climate change is real, significant, and human-caused
Downplaying climate change.target_belief_change: Climate change is natural, insignificant, not human-caused, or manageable without major intervention
Downplaying climate change.persuasive_strategy: Systematic factual denial — challenging each component of scientific consensus
Downplaying climate change.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS super-claims negating Ding 2011 beliefs ('it's real', 'it's us', 'it's bad'); Harris_2023 type-a across multiple factual dimensions; Kahan_2007 for identity component
Downplaying climate change.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative minimises climate change across multiple dimensions. Each sub-narrative targets a different factual belief about climate science.

Climate cycles are natural.tom_note: Targets the belief 'it's us' — claims warming is a natural cyclical phenomenon
Climate cycles are natural.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS negates 'it's us' (Ding 2011); Harris_2023 type-a: false causal attribution to natural cycles

Weather suggests the trend is global coo.tom_note: Targets the belief 'it's real' — uses local/short-term data to deny global warming trend
Weather suggests the trend is global coo.grounding: Cook_2020: cherry picking (FLICC) + CARDS negates 'it's real'; Wilbur_2025: anchoring bias on cold weather events

Temperature increase does not have signi.tom_note: Targets the belief 'it's bad' — claims temperature changes are too small to matter
Temperature increase does not have signi.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS negates 'it's bad' (Ding 2011); Bondarenko_2020: information restructuring (re-scaling the phenomenon)

CO2 concentrations are too small to have.tom_note: Targets the belief 'it's us' — claims human CO2 is negligible relative to natural sources
CO2 concentrations are too small to have.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS negates 'it's us'; Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about CO2 proportions

Human activities do not impact climate c.tom_note: Directly targets the belief 'it's us' — the most explicit denial of human causation
Human activities do not impact climate c.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS super-claim 'It's not us' (Ding 2011); Harris_2023 type-a: producing false belief about causation

Ice is not melting.tom_note: Targets the belief 'it's real' — denies observable physical evidence of warming
Ice is not melting.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS negates 'it's real'; cherry picking (FLICC) — selecting data showing stable ice in specific regions

Sea levels are not rising.tom_note: Targets the belief 'it's bad' — denies one of the most salient consequences of warming
Sea levels are not rising.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS negates 'it's bad'; Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about sea level data

Humans and nature will adapt to the chan.tom_note: Provides reassurance to reduce climate anxiety — 'we'll be fine' is an emotional comfort, not a factual claim
Humans and nature will adapt to the chan.grounding: Didi decision: emotional (reassurance/comfort) rather than epistemic; Wilbur_2025: countering loss aversion with comforting frame; still connects to Campbell_Kay_2014 solution aversion but the mechanism is emotional reassurance

Questioning the measurements and science.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader trusts scientific methods, data, and consensus on climate change
Questioning the measurements and science.target_belief_change: Scientific methods are flawed, data is unreliable or fabricated, and the scientific community is biased
Questioning the measurements and science.persuasive_strategy: Meta-epistemic attack — undermining the knowledge-production process itself
Questioning the measurements and science.grounding: Harris_2023 type-b (preventing true beliefs by destroying evidence base); Sperber_2010; Cook_2020: FLICC 'fake experts' + 'conspiracy'
Questioning the measurements and science.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks the scientific process, not specific findings. Distinguish from 'Downplaying' (denies findings) — this denies the process.

Methodologies/metrics used are unreliabl.tom_note: Attacks the measurement process — claims instruments, methods, or statistical approaches are flawed
Methodologies/metrics used are unreliabl.grounding: Cook_2020: FLICC 'impossible expectations' (demanding perfection from methodology); Harris_2023 type-b; annotation guidelines: often co-occurs with 'Data shows no increase'

Data shows no temperature increase.tom_note: Claims the data itself contradicts warming — targets beliefs about empirical evidence
Data shows no temperature increase.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about temperature data; Cook_2020: cherry picking specific datasets; annotation guidelines: often co-occurs with 'Methodologies unreliable'

Greenhouse effect/carbon dioxide do not.tom_note: Denies the fundamental causal mechanism of climate science — targets beliefs about physics of warming
Greenhouse effect/carbon dioxide do not.grounding: Cook_2020: CARDS negates 'it's us' at the mechanism level; Harris_2023 type-a: false belief about atmospheric physics

Scientific community is unreliable.tom_note: Attacks the character and integrity of scientists — frames them as biased, corrupt, or conspiratorial
Scientific community is unreliable.grounding: EntityFramingST1: scientists as Antagonist (Deceiver, Conspirator); Pauli_2022: misuse of ethos — attacking source character; Cook_2020: FLICC 'conspiracy' (alleging organised scientific fraud)

Criticism of climate movement.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may respect or sympathise with climate activists
Criticism of climate movement.target_belief_change: Climate activists are alarmist, corrupt, or personally objectionable — the movement is not credible
Criticism of climate movement.persuasive_strategy: Character attack on collective actors — delegitimising via ad hominem and corruption framing
Criticism of climate movement.grounding: EntityFramingST1 (activists as Antagonist); Pauli_2022 misuse of ethos; Cook_2020 attacking the messenger
Criticism of climate movement.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks climate activists and the movement. Look for language calling activists alarmist, corrupt, or for personal attacks on key figures.

Climate movement is alarmist.tom_note: Frames activists as irresponsible — exaggerating severity for dramatic effect undermines their credibility
Climate movement is alarmist.grounding: Pauli_2022: misuse of ethos (credibility attack); EntityFramingST1: activists as Antagonist (Deceiver — exaggerating); annotation guidelines: same example text used for 'alarmist' and 'corrupt'

Climate movement is corrupt.tom_note: Frames activists as driven by ulterior motives — money, power, or ideological agenda rather than genuine concern
Climate movement is corrupt.grounding: EntityFramingST1: activists as Antagonist (Corrupt); Pauli_2022: misuse of ethos (attacking motives); annotation guidelines: same example as 'alarmist' — genuine ambiguity

Ad hominem attacks on key activists.tom_note: Personal attacks on individuals (Greta Thunberg, etc.) — targets the messenger to discredit the message
Ad hominem attacks on key activists.grounding: Pauli_2022: explicit ad hominem — attacking person not argument; Cook_2020: FLICC attacking the messenger

Controversy about green technologies.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may view renewable energy positively as necessary for addressing climate change
Controversy about green technologies.target_belief_change: Green technologies are dangerous, unreliable, unaffordable, or worse than alternatives
Controversy about green technologies.persuasive_strategy: Technology delegitimisation — attacking specific solutions to undermine feasibility of action
Controversy about green technologies.grounding: Campbell_Kay_2014 solution aversion; Cook_2020 CARDS 'solutions won't work'; ARTT_2022 manufacturing doubt
Controversy about green technologies.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative attacks specific green technologies. Look for claims that renewables are dangerous, unreliable, or too costly.

Renewable energy is dangerous.tom_note: Triggers safety fears about renewable technology — frames green tech as threatening rather than beneficial
Renewable energy is dangerous.grounding: Wilbur_2025: emotional contagion (safety fear); ARTT_2022: evoking emotion; Campbell_Kay_2014: solution aversion amplified by perceived danger

Renewable energy is unreliable.tom_note: Factual claim about technology dependability — targets beliefs about practical viability
Renewable energy is unreliable.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: (exaggerated) beliefs about unreliability; Cook_2020: impossible expectations (demanding 100% reliability)

Renewable energy is costly.tom_note: Cost-based factual claim — targets beliefs about economic viability of green transition
Renewable energy is costly.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: (exaggerated) cost beliefs; Campbell_Kay_2014: solution aversion — costs make the solution threatening; overlaps with emotional (economic anxiety)

Nuclear energy is not climate friendly.tom_note: Factual claim about nuclear energy's climate credentials — targets beliefs about whether nuclear should count as clean energy
Nuclear energy is not climate friendly.grounding: Harris_2023 type-a: challenging the classification of nuclear as climate-friendly; complex because anti-nuclear sentiment spans both environmental and safety concerns

Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may accept that climate action is driven by genuine concern and science
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.target_belief_change: Climate action is driven by hidden agendas of global elites pursuing control or profit
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.persuasive_strategy: Conspiratorial framing — identical cognitive structure to URW hidden plots
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.grounding: Cook_2020: conspiracy theory (FLICC technique); ARTT_2022 conspiratorial reasoning; Bondarenko_2020
Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerf.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative invokes elite conspiracies behind climate action. Look for references to WEF, global elites, or hidden agendas behind climate policy.

Blaming global elites.tom_note: Attributes climate agenda to identifiable elite actors (WEF, Bill Gates) — provides a concrete conspiratorial causal agent
Blaming global elites.grounding: Cook_2020: conspiracy theories with 'questionable-motive assumptions'; Harris_2023 type-a: false beliefs about who drives climate policy; overlaps with identity (elites as out-group)

Climate agenda has hidden motives.tom_note: Claims the true purpose of climate action is concealed — population control, profit, political power
Climate agenda has hidden motives.grounding: Cook_2020: conspiracy 'self-sealing reasoning'; Harris_2023 type-a: false beliefs about policy motivation; Maillat_Oswald_2009: manipulating assumptions about hidden agency

Amplifying Climate Fears.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader may feel complacent or hopeful about addressing climate change
Amplifying Climate Fears.target_belief_change: Catastrophic climate consequences are imminent, irreversible, and potentially civilisation-ending
Amplifying Climate Fears.persuasive_strategy: Fear amplification through doomsday scenario framing
Amplifying Climate Fears.grounding: Wilbur_2025 emotional contagion + loss aversion; mirrors URW 'Amplifying war-related fears'; Cook_2020 inverts 'there's hope' belief
Amplifying Climate Fears.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative amplifies climate anxiety beyond what science supports. Look for language about imminent catastrophe, uninhabitability, or futility of action.

Earth will be uninhabitable soon.tom_note: Existential fear at planetary scale — frames near-term uninhabitability as certain
Earth will be uninhabitable soon.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion at existential scale; ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (existential dread)

Amplifying existing fears of global warm.tom_note: Generalised fear amplification — takes real climate concerns and escalates them beyond scientific support
Amplifying existing fears of global warm.grounding: Wilbur_2025: emotional contagion; Maillat_Oswald_2009: exploiting cognitive optimism (once fear frame is activated, it constrains interpretation)

Doomsday scenarios for humans.tom_note: Civilisation-ending scenarios — mass extinction, societal collapse as direct consequences of climate change
Doomsday scenarios for humans.grounding: Wilbur_2025: loss aversion at civilisational scale; ARTT_2022: evoking emotion at maximum intensity

Whatever we do it is already too late.tom_note: Induces fatalistic despair — specifically targets the belief that action is worthwhile (inverts Cook's 'there's hope')
Whatever we do it is already too late.grounding: Cook_2020: inverts Ding 2011 belief 'there's hope'; Campbell_Kay_2014: a perverse form of solution aversion — not rejecting solutions because they're costly, but because they're futile; ARTT_2022: evoking emotion (despair)

Green policies are geopolitical instrume.presupposed_reader_belief: Reader (especially in developing countries) may view climate cooperation as mutually beneficial
Green policies are geopolitical instrume.target_belief_change: Climate policies are tools of Western domination — neo-colonialism in green clothing
Green policies are geopolitical instrume.persuasive_strategy: Anti-colonial reframing — recasting climate cooperation as power asymmetry
Green policies are geopolitical instrume.grounding: Kahan_2007 identity-protective cognition (group-level threat); Feygina_2010 system justification; Stanley_2015 ideological presuppositions
Green policies are geopolitical instrume.tom_note_for_prompt: This narrative frames green policies as geopolitical tools. Look for language about neo-colonialism, exploitation, or climate policy serving Western interests at developing nations' expense.

Climate-related international relations.tom_note: Frames climate diplomacy as power exploitation — developed vs. developing nations as in-group/out-group
Climate-related international relations.grounding: Kahan_2007: identity-protective cognition activated by perceived group-level economic threat; post-colonial critique framing

Green activities are a form of neo-colon.tom_note: Explicitly invokes colonial frame — green investment as modern colonisation of developing nations
Green activities are a form of neo-colon.grounding: Kahan_2007: identity-protective cognition; Feygina_2010: defending existing economic structures against climate-justified restructuring; strongest identity framing in CC taxonomy

H1_mechanism_distribution.description: URW narratives span all four mechanisms roughly equally, while CC narratives are overwhelmingly epistemic. This reflects the different nature of the two propaganda domains: geopolitical propaganda uses the full cognitive toolkit, while climate denial primarily operates through factual claims.
H1_mechanism_distribution.testable: Compare mechanism distributions between domains using chi-square test.

H2_confusion_shares_mechanism.description: Confused sub-narrative pairs (from bis(TCM)) should disproportionately share the same primary mechanism. Models can distinguish across mechanisms (fear vs. credibility attack) but struggle within mechanisms (two different epistemic claims).
H2_confusion_shares_mechanism.testable: Compare within-mechanism vs. cross-mechanism confusion rates using permutation test on bis(TCM) off-diagonal entries.

H3_multi_mechanism_narratives_harder.description: Narratives with multiple mechanisms (e.g., 'Discrediting Ukraine' spans moral/epistemic/identity/emotional) should have higher internal confusion among their sub-narratives than single-mechanism narratives (e.g., 'Amplifying war fears' is purely emotional).
H3_multi_mechanism_narratives_harder.testable: Compute within-narrative sub-narrative confusion for multi-mechanism vs. single-mechanism narratives.

H4_tom_chain_helps_within_mechanism.description: ToM reasoning chain should primarily improve classification of sub-narratives that share a mechanism with other sub-narratives under different parent narratives (cross-narrative, same-mechanism confusion). It should have less effect on cross-mechanism confusion (already handled by surface features).
H4_tom_chain_helps_within_mechanism.testable: Compare ToM vs. baseline improvement on same-mechanism confused pairs vs. different-mechanism confused pairs.

_tcm_cross_reference.description: Cross-reference of empirical confused pairs (from bis(TCM) on 435 existing predictions) with ToM mechanism assignments
_tcm_cross_reference.finding: The 10 most confused narrative pairs split into two qualitatively distinct categories based on ToM mechanisms

same_mechanism_pairs.description: 5 pairs sharing the same primary mechanism — these appear to be GENUINE COGNITIVE CONFUSION where the model cannot distinguish two claims operating through the same belief-manipulation channel

pairs.interpretation: Both target trust in who controls events
Both trigger fear about consequences for the reader
Both shift blame/distrust toward Western actors
Both undermine trust in authoritative institutions
Both challenge scientific consensus through factual counter-claims

cross_mechanism_pairs.description: 5 pairs with different primary mechanisms — these appear to be THEMATIC CO-OCCURRENCE where narratives serve complementary propaganda functions in the same documents

pairs.interpretation: Russia-winning predictions co-occur with Russia-sympathy framing (COMPLEMENTARY)
NATO strength articles also discuss escalation risk (SAME TOPIC)
Alarmism criticism overlaps with actual alarm (MIRROR IMAGES)
Counter-claims and fear-amplification co-occur in denial texts
Discrediting adversary while praising own side (COMPLEMENTARY)

_tcm_cross_reference.updated_hypothesis_H2: Refined from 'confused pairs share mechanisms' to: bis(TCM) captures TWO distinct confusion types — (1) same-mechanism cognitive confusion (model can't distinguish two claims operating through the same belief channel) and (2) cross-mechanism thematic co-occurrence (complementary narratives that appear together in the same propaganda texts). The ToM mechanism annotation allows us to disentangle these for the first time.
